content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 24, 2012

Lori J. Barker, City Attorney
Office of the City Attorney
P O Box 3420
Chico, CA 95927

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our file No. A-11-241

Dear Ms. Barker:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note this letter is based on the facts
presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder
of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) There are other bodies
of law, separate and apart from the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions, that may apply to the
situation you have described. We urge you to check with the Attorney General's office to
determine whether any other laws are applicable in light of the facts you present.

QUESTION

Are members of the City of Chico's Sustainability Task Force (the "Task Force") "public
officials" under the Act and therefore subject to the Act's disclosure and disqualification
provisions?

CONCLUSION

No. Even though there is a mixed history where some substantive Task Force
recommendations were approved by the City Council as submitted, while others were approved
but only after significant amendments or modifications were made, the Task Force does not
possess decision-making authority for purposes of the Act. Therefore, Task Force members are
not "public officials" under the Act.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-11-241
Page No. 2

FACTS

The City Council for the City of Chico (the "City Council") has asked you, as City
Attorney, to request advice regarding the applicability of the Act's conflict-of-interest
provisions to members of the Task Force.

The Task Force was created by the City Council in December 2006 for the purpose of
working on the twelve points contained in the U.S. Mayors Climate Protection Agreement and
make recommendations for its implementation. Each council member appoints one Task Force
member and eight additional members are appointed by the Mayor, with two to represent the
local university, environmental groups, the business community and the community at large.
More specific work items were assigned to the Task Force in the three biennial work plans
proposed by the Task Force and approved by the City Council, as well by the specific referral of
an item to the Task Force. Work plan items were intended to be and were, in fact, forwarded as
recommendations to the City Council for approval.

The Task Force has never been granted any authority to make a final decision as to any
matter or to appropriate funds, spend money, hire staff or consultants, or enter into contracts.
Also, the Task Force has never been granted any authority to compel or prevent a final decision.
The Task Force has made recommendations to the City Council approximately twice a year since
early 2007. In addition, the Task Force provided input during 2009 to city staff and consultants
in connection with a general plan update that was in progress at that time. The Task Force has
made recommendations to decisionmakers and the decisionmakers have taken actions in
response to such recommendations.

You have provided a timeline setting forth the dates recommendations were made, the
nature of those recommendations and City Council or staff actions made in response to the
recommendations. Following is our summary of the timeline:

• On December 19, 2006, the Task Force was formed by the City Council.

• On November 6, 2007, the Task Force presented and recommended to the City
Council the first work plan. The City Council approved all the recommendations
as proposed.

• On May 6, 2008, the Task Force submitted a recommendation that the City
Council adopt a policy prohibiting the use of City funds to purchase single-
serving water bottles. The recommendation was adopted by the City Council with
significant modifications.

• On September 2, 2008, the Task Force made four recommendations in connection
with an updated work plan, which was approved by the City Council with no
substantial changes.

File No. A-11-241
Page No. 3

• On February 3, 2009, the Task Force made a recommendation on a matter the
City Council had specifically asked the Task Force to address. After detailed
discussions, the recommendation was adopted but with additional directions for
the City staff to conduct further research.

• On various dates during 2009 and 2010, the Task Force, as well as other parties,
provided input to City staff and consultants regarding proposed updates to the
City's General Plan. Staff took Task Force input into consideration, but such
input was not routinely accepted or incorporated.

• On June 2, 2009, the City Council approved without modification the second
work plan submitted by the Task Force.

• On April 20, 2010, the City Council considered a recommendation by the Task
Force that the city's municipal code be updated. The recommendation was
generally accepted, with some substantive modifications. The Task Force did not
participate in the review of the amended ordinance after its initial
recommendation to the City Council.

• On September 7, 2010, the City Council approved the Task Force's
recommendation regarding an update on one of the work plan items. The City
Council also directed the Task Force to proceed with additional tasks relating to
that item.

• On March 1, 2011, the City Council approved a Task Force recommendation for
adoption of a general policy statement extending producer liability, which was the
subject of one of the resolutions adopted at the meeting at which the Task Force
was created. This policy statement does not commit the City to any action or
expenditure of any funds.

• On September 20, 2011, the City Council approved the 2011-2012 work plan
recommended by the Task Force as presented.

Mark Sorensen, a City Council member, has written to us stating his understanding that
Task Force members have been "public officials" from inception of the Task Force and that
since 2007, Task Force recommendations have been regularly approved by the City Council and
staff without significant amendment or modification. You have authorized us to consider points
he makes and facts he believes support his contentions. However, where his facts are
inconsistent with those you have provided, we must accept your facts for purposes of our

File No. A-11-241
Page No. 4


analysis. (As stated above, the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders
advice.)2

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Section 87100
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which official has a financial
interest. In order to determine whether members of the Task Force make, participate in making,
otherwise use their official positions to influence a governmental decision, we must first consider
whether they are "public officials" within the meaning of Section 87100.

I. Are members of the Task Force "public officials"?

Under Section 82048(a), a public official includes a member, officer, employee or
consultant of a local government agency. Section 82041 provides that "local government
agency" means a county, city or district, or any other local or regional political subdivision,
bureau, office, board, commission or other agency of a county, city or district. If members of the
Task Force make, participate in making or use their official positions to influence a
governmental decision, the Task Force is an agency of the City. Therefore, our analysis turns to
the question you have raised: whether members of the Task Force make, participate in making
or use their official positions to influence a governmental decision.

II. Making, participating in making, or using one's official position to influence a
governmental decision.

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when,
acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant intervening substantive
review, the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker
regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to
use his or her official position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the
official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her
agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)

To determine whether members of the Task Force make governmental decisions, we first
look at Regulation 18701(a)(1), which defines "member" as follows:

2 The only disputed fact that we find relevant to our analysis is that Mr. Sorenson states that the Task Force
selected vendors for certain work. You state that the Task Force has no authority to hire staff or consultants. As
stated above, we must accept your facts in this matter.

File No. A-11-241
Page No. 5

"Member" shall include, but not be limited to, salaried or
unsalaried members of committees, boards or commissions with
policymaking authority. (A) A committee, board or commission
possesses decisionmaking authority whenever: (i) It may make a
final governmental decision; (ii) It may compel a governmental
decision; or it may prevent a governmental decision either by
reason of an exclusive power to initiate the decision or by reason
of a veto that may not be overridden; or (iii) It makes substantive
recommendations that are, and over an extended period of time
have been, regularly approved without significant amendment or
modification by another public official or governmental agency."

"(A) A committee, board or commission possesses decisionmaking
authority whenever:

"(i) It may make a final governmental decision;

"(ii) It may compel a governmental decision; or it may prevent a
governmental decision either by reason of an exclusive power to
initiate the decision or by reason of a veto that may not be
overridden; or

"(iii) It makes substantive recommendations that are, and over an
extended period of time have been, regularly approved without
significant amendment or modification by another public official
or governmental agency."

"(B) A committee, board, or commission does not possess
decisionmaking authority under subsection (a)(1)(A)(1) of this
regulation if it is formed for the sole purpose of researching a topic
and preparing a report or recommendation for submission to
another governmental body that has final decisionmaking
authority." (Emphasis added.)

You state that the Task Force was created to work on the twelve points contained in the
U.S. Mayors Climate Protection Agreement (the "Climate Protection Agreement"), which was
adopted by resolution of the City Council, and to make recommendations for implementation of
work plans contemplated by the Climate Protection Agreement. Since its formation, the Task
Force has made recommendations relating to these matters in connection with amendments to the
General Plan and the Municipal Code.

Specific work items to be undertaken by the Task Force have been further defined (i) in
three biennial work plans that have been proposed by the Task Force and approved by the City

File No. A-11-241
Page No. 6


Council, and (ii) by the referral of specific items to the Task Force by the City Council. Work
plan items were intended to be forwarded as recommendations to the City Council, which has the
final authority over whether to approve those recommendations.

A. Making a final governmental decision.

Under the Act, a public official makes a governmental decision when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position:

"(1) Votes on a matter;
"(2) Appoints a person;
"(3) Obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of
action;
"(4) Enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency . . .." (Regulation 18702.1(a).)

In prior letters, we have advised that members of a body make, participate in making or
influence a governmental decision if they are authorized to adopt rules, rates and regulations for
the administration and management of a parking district, have the authority to hire, fire or
appoint personnel or outside consultants, purchase supplies or enter into contracts. (See Advice
Letters: Ewing, No. A-89-480; Amen, No. A-88-304, Glacken, No. I-92-265a and Weaver, No.
A-03-225).

Nothing in the facts or materials you have provided indicates that the Task Force has
authority to adopt rules, rates or regulations, purchase supplies, hire, appoint or fire personnel, or
hire outside consultants. Thus, it does not appear that the Task Force has direct authority to
make a governmental decision.

B. Compelling or preventing a governmental decision.

Even though the Task Force lacks direct authority to make final governmental decisions, if
the Task Force may compel a governmental decision or prevent a governmental decision either
by reason of an exclusive power to initiate the decision or by reason of a veto that may not be
overridden, the Task Force would have decisionmaking authority under Regulation
18701(a)(1)(A)(ii). Nothing in your facts suggests that the Task Force may compel a
governmental decision or prevent a governmental decision either by reason of an exclusive
power to initiate the decision or by reason of a veto that may not be overridden.

C. Making substantive recommendations that are, and over an extended period of
time, have been regularly approved without significant amendment or modification.

If the recommendations of a body have a significant impact upon the ultimate outcome of
a decision, the body is considered to have decisionmaking authority and is therefore not solely

File No. A-11-241
Page No. 7

advisory. (Regulation 18701(a)(1)(A)(ii); In re Roman (1987) 10 FPPC Ops. 1.) We have
advised on several occasions that if there is a history or track record of "rubber stamping" an
advisory body's recommendations, the advisory body is no longer purely advisory and will be
considered to have decisionmaking authority. (See, e.g., Baird Advice Letter, No. A-94-299;
Czech Advice Letter, No. A-91-503; Woodbury Advice Letter, A-90-665; Paley Advice Letter,
A-90-583; Korade Advice Letter, A-89-715; Ball Advice Letter, I-89-671.)

We have advised that where:

The decisionmaker adopted, without amendment or modification, all of the thirty plans
recommended by an advisory body over an eleven-year period, the body was no longer
purely advisory. (Calabrese Advice letter (No. I-08067.);

The decisionmaker followed an advisory body's recommendations only once over a four-
year period, the body remained purely advisory. (Simon Advice Letter, No. I-09-076);

The recommendations of an advisory body are first reviewed by another person or entity
such as a city staff member, and that person then makes recommendations directly to the
decisionmaker, the person has made "substantive, intervening reviews" and the advisory
body remains purely advisory. (Paley Advice Letter, No. A-90-583; Andriese Advice
Letter, No. A-02-276.)

Recommendations the City Council or staff approved with and without significant amendment or
modification.

You have indicated that between November 2007 and September 2011, the City Council
approved three proposed work plans recommended by the Task Force, as well as an update to the
first plan, without modification. Two other substantive decisions made by the City Council
during this time period based on recommendations of the Task Force are (1) choosing how to
respond to a rule proposed by the Butte County Air Quality Management District in February of
2009, and (2) approving a proposed update on the development of the Climate Action Plan, one
of the work plan items September 2010. In both matters, the City Council approved the Task
Force recommendations, with only minor changes.³

Also during this period, the Task Force made recommendations regarding City policies,
rather than action items. First, the City Council voted to adopt a policy prohibiting use of City
funds to provide single-serving water bottles for employees. This recommendation was adopted
after significant exceptions were added by the City Council. Second, the City Council voted to

³ In deciding to adopt the recommended response to the Butte County letter, the City Council directed staff
to research related matters that had no effect on the City Council's decision. In deciding to adopt the
recommendations for developing an updated Climate Action Plan, the City Council directed staff to prepare a cost
analysis and include a section pertaining to sustainability impacts, if applicable, on future staff reports. These
directions to not appear to affected the City Council's decision.

File No. A-11-241
Page No. 8


issue a policy statement in support of "extended producer liability."4 This recommendation was
approved by the City Council without amendment or modification.

     You indicate that the Task Force provided input, solicited by staff, for updating portions
of the City's general plan and that during the process of drafting amendments, staff also received
and considered input from other individuals. You also state that "the Task Force did not direct
specific action to be taken in regard to drafts produced or provide recommendations that were
routinely accepted and incorporated." Under these facts, it does not appear that staff or the City
Council approved Task Force recommendations regarding General Plan amendments.

     You also state that amendments made to the Municipal Code by the City Council "were
largely the same as proposed by the Task Force, although there were some substantive
modifications." Because substantive modifications were made, the City Council's approval of
the recommendations was not made "without significant amendment or modification."

     It appears that the City Council has made ten decisions based on Task Force
recommendations over a five year period. Only five recommendations were adopted by the City
Council without significant amendment or modification. Under these facts, we conclude that the
Task Force remains a purely advisory body. However, please note that this conclusion could
change should the City Council engage in a practice of approving substantive recommendations
submitted by the Task Force, over an extended period of time, without significant amendment or
modification by the City Council or City staff.

     If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

                                                           Sincerely,

                                                           Zackery P. Morazzini
                                                           General Counsel

                                                     By:    Valentina Joyce
                                                            Counsel, Legal Division
VJ:jgl




     4 The resolutions supporting "extended producer liability" urged the California Integrated Waste
Management Board and the Department of Toxic Substances to take certain actions in the management of toxic
substances and products and manufacturers to share responsibility for disposal of toxic substances. The City Council
also authorized and instructed staff to advocate for legislation on these matters.

such as a city staff member, and that person then makes recommendations directly to the
decisionmaker, the person has made "substantive, intervening reviews" and the advisory
body remains purely advisory. (Paley Advice Letter, No. 4-90-5 83; Andriese Advice
Letter, No. 4-02-276.)
Recommendations the City Council or staff approved with and without signiftcant amendment or
modification.
You have indicated that between November 2007 and September 2011, the City Council
approved three proposed work plans recommended by the Task Force, as well as an update to the
first plan, without modification. Two other substantive decisions made by the City Council
during this time period based on recommendations of the Task Force are (1) choosing how to
respond to a rule proposed by the Butte County Air Quality Management District in February of
2009, and (2) approving a proposed update on the development of the Climate Action plan, one
of the work plan items September 2010. tn both m^atters, the City Council approved the Task
Force recorlmendations, with only minor changes.3
Also during this period, the Task Force made recommendations regarding City policies,
rather than action items. First, the City Council voted to adopt a policy prohibiting useof City
funds to provide single-serving water bottles for employees. This recommendation was adopied
after significant exceptions were added by the City Council. Second, the Citypouncil voted to
3 In deciding to adopt the recommended response to the Butte County letter, the City Council directed staff
to research related matters that had no effect on the City Council's decision. In deciding to adopt the
recommendations for developing an updated Climate Action Plan, the City Council directed staff to prepare a cost
analysis and include a section pertaining to sustainability impacts, if applicable, on future staff reporis. These
directions to not appear to affected the City Council's decision.
issue a policy statement in support of "extended producer liability."a This recommendation was
approved by the City Council without amendment or modification.
You indicate that the Task Force provided input, solicited by staff, for updating portions
of the City's general plan and that during the process of drafting amendments, staff also received
and considered input from other individuals. You also state that "the Task Force did not direct
specific action to be taken in regard to drafts produced or provide recommendations that were
routinely accepted and incorporated." Under these facts, it does not appear that staff or the City
Council approved Task Force recommendations regarding General Plan amendments.
You also state that amendments made to the Municipal Code by the City Council "were
largely the same as proposed by the Task Force, although there were some substantive
modifications." Because substantive modifications were made, the City Council's approval of
the recommendations was not made "without significant amendment or modification."
It appears that the City Council has made ten decisions based on Task Force
recommendations over a five year period. Only five recommendations were adopted by the City
Council without significant amendment or modification. Under these facts, we conclude that the
Task Force remains a purely advisory body. However, please note that this conclusion could
change should the City Council engage in a practice of approving substantive recommendations
submitted by the Task Force, over an extended period of time, without significant amendment or
modification by the City Council or City staff.

embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 January 24, 2012 Lori J. Barker, City Attorney Office of the City Attorney P O Box 3420 Chico, CA 95927 Re: Your Request for Advice Our file No. A-11-241 Dear Ms. Barker: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) There are other bodies of law, separate and apart from the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions, that may apply to the situation you have described. We urge you to check with the Attorney General's office to determine whether any other laws are applicable in light of the facts you present. QUESTION Are members of the City of Chico's Sustainability Task Force (the "Task Force") "public officials" under the Act and therefore subject to the Act's disclosure and disqualification provisions? CONCLUSION No. Even though there is a mixed history where some substantive Task Force recommendations were approved by the City Council as submitted, while others were approved but only after significant amendments or modifications were made, the Task Force does not possess decision-making authority for purposes of the Act. Therefore, Task Force members are not "public officials" under the Act. ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-11-241 Page No. 2 FACTS The City Council for the City of Chico (the "City Council") has asked you, as City Attorney, to request advice regarding the applicability of the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions to members of the Task Force. The Task Force was created by the City Council in December 2006 for the purpose of working on the twelve points contained in the U.S. Mayors Climate Protection Agreement and make recommendations for its implementation. Each council member appoints one Task Force member and eight additional members are appointed by the Mayor, with two to represent the local university, environmental groups, the business community and the community at large. More specific work items were assigned to the Task Force in the three biennial work plans proposed by the Task Force and approved by the City Council, as well by the specific referral of an item to the Task Force. Work plan items were intended to be and were, in fact, forwarded as recommendations to the City Council for approval. The Task Force has never been granted any authority to make a final decision as to any matter
